claims.claim_text: The CKA exam allocates 30% of its domains to troubleshooting, and candidates have 2 hours to complete the performance-based tasks.
claims.rationale: The Linux Foundation CKA page explicitly lists Troubleshooting at 30% and states the exam is a 2-hour performance-based test.

claims.claim_text: The module states that the CKA environment often permits shell aliases.
claims.anchor_text: The CKA environment often permits aliases, and this module uses `k` as shorthand after defining it once here.
claims.suggested_rewrite: This module uses `k` as shorthand after defining it once here.
claims.rationale: No allowlisted official exam source in scope here confirms alias support as a stable property of the exam environment.

claims.claim_text: The module says a Pending pod has no useful container logs because the container has not started.
claims.span_hint: section: 1.3 Isolate by Layer, Not by Guess
claims.anchor_text: If a pod is still `Pending`, there is no useful container log because the container has not started.
claims.suggested_rewrite: If a pod is still `Pending`, inspect `describe` and Events first because the application container may not have started yet, so logs may be absent or not yet useful.
claims.rationale: Pending includes multiple pre-start stages, so the absolute no-logs statement is broader than the upstream lifecycle semantics even though the troubleshooting advice is directionally correct.

claims.claim_text: A pod can be Running but still excluded from Service traffic until readiness succeeds and endpoints are published.
claims.span_hint: section: 1.4 Inspect Evidence in an Order That Matches the Lifecycle
claims.rationale: The pod lifecycle and Service docs distinguish phase from readiness, and the service debugging docs show endpoint publication as a separate step.

claims.claim_text: If the API server is down most kubectl operations fail, if the scheduler is down new pods stay unscheduled, and if the controller manager is down controllers stop reconciling desired state.
claims.span_hint: section: 2.1 Control Plane Components and Their Failure Shapes
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes components overview defines the API server, scheduler, and controller manager responsibilities directly.

claims.claim_text: The kubelet manages containers and probes for a running Pod, pod networking is provided by the pod network implementation, and Service proxying can be handled by kube-proxy or by the network implementation depending on cluster configuration.
claims.span_hint: section: 2.2 Node Components and Their Failure Shapes
claims.rationale: The pod lifecycle and networking docs explicitly assign kubelet probe management and explain that pod networking and service proxying may be implemented by the network stack rather than kube-proxy alone.

claims.claim_text: kubectl describe is high-value early evidence because it surfaces scheduling, volume, image, container-state, condition, and event information that logs alone do not provide.
claims.rationale: The debug-running-pod guide uses describe output and Events to diagnose startup and scheduling failures before process-level logs.

claims.claim_text: When a container has restarted, current logs may miss the actual crash and kubectl logs --previous is needed; multi-container pods also require explicit container selection.
claims.rationale: The kubectl logs reference documents --previous, and the logging architecture docs explain previous-instance retrieval and container selection.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes Events are short-lived, best-effort signals rather than durable logging records.
claims.rationale: The glossary states that Events have limited retention and should be treated as informative supplemental data, while the debug guide shows their operational role.

claims.claim_text: On kubeadm-managed clusters, control-plane components are written as static pod manifests under /etc/kubernetes/manifests, run in kube-system, and local etcd is also managed as a static pod.
claims.span_hint: section: 3.7 Node and Control Plane Checks
claims.rationale: The kubeadm implementation details page explicitly documents the static manifest path, kube-system placement, and local etcd static pod behavior.

claims.claim_text: Pod phase is only a high-level summary: Pending includes scheduling and setup time, Running does not mean fully healthy, and Unknown typically reflects node communication problems.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 4: Reading Pod Status Without Being Fooled
claims.rationale: The pod lifecycle docs define phase semantics and explicitly warn that phase is not a comprehensive health verdict.

claims.claim_text: DNS resolution of a Service name only proves name-to-virtual-IP lookup; successful routing also depends on selector-produced endpoints and correct targetPort mapping to the backend listener.
claims.rationale: The DNS, Service, and debug-service docs separately document service DNS, selector-to-EndpointSlice behavior, and targetPort mapping.

claims.claim_text: NetworkPolicy works by selecting pods and then allowing only the ingress or egress traffic matched by its rules; applicable policies combine additively.
claims.rationale: The NetworkPolicy docs explicitly describe pod selection, allow rules, and additive policy behavior.

claims.claim_text: A pod that depends on persistent storage can be blocked either before PVC/PV binding or later during attach and mount, so PVC Pending and pod FailedMount point to different storage layers.
claims.rationale: Persistent volume docs explain claim and volume binding phases, while pod-level debugging evidence is where mount failures surface.

claims.claim_text: The module states that BusyBox is commonly sufficient for DNS and simple HTTP checks in restricted exam environments.
claims.span_hint: section: 3.6 In-Pod Network and DNS Tests
claims.anchor_text: In restricted exam environments, BusyBox is commonly enough for DNS and simple HTTP checks.
claims.suggested_rewrite: In a restricted environment, a minimal debug image is often enough for DNS and simple HTTP checks.
claims.rationale: The operational lesson is to test DNS and HTTP from inside the cluster; the specific BusyBox-in-exam-environment claim is too environment-specific for the current allowlisted source set.

further_reading.title: Debug Running Pods
further_reading.why_relevant: Best primary reference for describe, Events, logs, and pod-level inspection order.

further_reading.title: Debug Services
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers the practical service-debug path from DNS to EndpointSlices to backend pod checks.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes API Health Endpoints
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful for the module’s control-plane triage guidance, especially /readyz and verbose health checks.

notes: Ignored analogies, diagrams, code blocks, quiz prompts/answers, and exercise manifests per schema; this seed only captures prose claims that materially affect factual understanding or exam-specific assertions.
